items.title: Fix broken or placeholder links across HTML pages — 30 LTD Bounty
items.body:
# Fix broken/placeholder links — 30 LTD Bounty (Tier 0)

**Tier:** 0 (anyone can claim)

## Task
Find and fix all \`href="#"\`, \`href="javascript:void(0)"\` placeholder links and dead \`href\` links across the public HTML pages.

## Scope
- **Files to check:** All \`.html\` files at root
- Link placeholders should either be removed, pointed to the correct destination, or converted to \`<button>\` if they trigger JS actions

## Requirements
1. Audit all HTML files for \`href="#"\` and \`href="javascript:void(0)"\`
2. For each: determine if it should link somewhere real, or be a \`<button>\` instead
3. Fix accordingly. If a link has a \`data-action\` handler, convert to \`<button>\`
4. List all changes in the PR description

## Definition of Done
- [ ] Zero \`href="#"\` that should be real links (navigational links that go nowhere)
- [ ] Action triggers converted from \`<a>\` to \`<button>\` where appropriate
- [ ] No broken navigation
- [ ] No console errors

## Codebase Verification Question
> To claim this bounty, answer: **How many HTML files are at the root of the repo? Name 5 of them. What tag does the platform use for click actions — \`<a onclick>\` or \`<button data-action>\`?**

## Estimated Time
1-2 hours.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] DebuggerPanel breakpointCount badge excludes exception breakpoints — count understates actual total
items.body:
### Project ide ### Description The DebuggerPanel's `breakpointCount` memo (lines 60-67) counts regular file breakpoints, function breakpoints, and data breakpoints, but does **not** include exception breakpoints. The BreakpointPanel (line 237-242) renders exception breakpoints alongside the other types, but they are invisible to the badge count. **Root cause (lines 60-67):** ```tsx const breakpointCount = createMemo(() => { let count = 0; for (const bps of Object.values(debug.state.breakpoints)) { count += bps.length; // file breakpoints } count += debug.state.functionBreakpoints.length; // function breakpoints count += debug.state.dataBreakpoints.length; // data breakpoints return count; // MISSING: debug.state.exceptionBreakpoints is not counted }); ``` The badge is displayed at line 102: ```tsx { label: "Breakpoints", icon: "circle-dot", badge: () => breakpointCount() || undefined } ``` ### Error Message ```shell N/A — logic bug, no runtime error ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell # DebuggerPanel.tsx lines 60-67 — breakpointCount memo: # Counts: breakpoints (by file) + functionBreakpoints + dataBreakpoints # Missing: exceptionBreakpoints # BreakpointPanel.tsx line 212 — exception breakpoints exist in state: const exceptionBps = () => debug.state.exceptionBreakpoints; # BreakpointPanel.tsx lines 237-242 — exception breakpoints are rendered: 0}>
{(eb) => }
``` ### System Information ```shell Cortex IDE: alpha OS: Microsoft Windows 11 Home Single Language Component: src/components/debugger/DebuggerPanel.tsx, lines 60-67 (breakpointCount), line 102 (badge display) ``` ### Screenshots ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE and start a debug session. 2. Set several file breakpoints (e.g., 3 breakpoints in different files). 3. Enable exception breakpoints: check both "Caught Exceptions" and "Uncaught Exceptions" in the Breakpoints panel. 4. Observe the "Breakpoints" sidebar section badge — it shows "3" instead of "5". 5. The BreakpointPanel shows all 5 breakpoints (2 exception + 3 file), but the badge only counts the 3 file breakpoints. ### Expected Behavior The Breakpoints badge should count ALL breakpoint types including exception breakpoints. With 3 file breakpoints and 2 exception breakpoints enabled, the badge should show "5". ### Actual Behavior The badge shows "3" because `breakpointCount()` at lines 60-67 only sums `breakpoints` (file), `functionBreakpoints`, and `dataBreakpoints` — it does not add `exceptionBreakpoints.length`. Exception breakpoints are rendered in the panel but excluded from the count.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] CodespacesPanel getStatusColor() missing ShuttingDown and Rebuilding cases — loading states show gray instead of warning color
items.body: ### Project ide ### Description The CodespacesPanel has an inconsistency between `getStatusColor()` (lines 20-37) and `isLoading()` (line 42). The `isLoading()` function correctly identifies `"ShuttingDown"` and `"Rebuilding"` as transitional/loading states (showing a spinner), but `getStatusColor()` has no case for these states — they fall through to the default case and display as gray (`var(--text-weak)`) instead of the warning color used by other loading states. **Root cause:** `getStatusColor()` maps `Starting`, `Provisioning`, `Queued` to `var(--warning)`, but `ShuttingDown` and `Rebuilding` are missing from this group despite being transitional loading states. ### Error Message ```shell N/A — logic/display bug ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell # Lines 20-37 — getStatusColor() switch cases: case "Starting": case "Provisioning": case "Queued": return "var(--warning)"; # Loading states → warning color case "Shutdown": # Only "Shutdown" (finished), NOT "ShuttingDown" case "Unavailable": return "var(--text-weaker)"; # ... default returns "var(--text-weak)" (gray) # Line 42 — isLoading() includes ShuttingDown and Rebuilding: const isLoading = () => ["Starting", "Provisioning", "Queued", "ShuttingDown", "Rebuilding"].includes(props.state); # Result: ShuttingDown/Rebuilding get spinner (isLoading=true) but gray color (default case) # Expected: warning color like Starting/Provisioning/Queued ``` ### System Information ```shell Cortex IDE: alpha OS: Microsoft Windows 11 Home Single Language Component: src/components/codespaces/CodespacesPanel.tsx, lines 20-37 (getStatusColor), line 42 (isLoading) ``` ### Screenshots ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE and navigate to the Codespaces panel. 2. Create or observe a codespace that is in the "ShuttingDown" state. 3. Observe that the status indicator shows a loading spinner (correct — isLoading recognizes it). 4. The status dot color is gray instead of the warning/orange color used for other loading states (Starting, Provisioning, Queued). 5. Same behavior for the "Rebuilding" state. ### Expected Behavior Codespaces in `ShuttingDown` and `Rebuilding` states should show the warning color (`var(--warning)`) like other transitional states (`Starting`, `Provisioning`, `Queued`), since they are recognized as loading states by `isLoading()` and display a spinner. ### Actual Behavior `ShuttingDown` and `Rebuilding` show a spinner (from `isLoading()`) but with a gray dot (from `getStatusColor()` default case) instead of the warning color. The `getStatusColor()` switch statement at lines 20-37 maps `Starting/Provisioning/Queued` to `var(--warning)` but has no case for `ShuttingDown` or `Rebuilding`.

**Tier:** 1 (Open Race) | **Reward:** 100 $FNDRY | **Timeout:** 72h

---

### Description

Set up the CI/CD pipeline for the SolFoundry monorepo — linting, testing, building, and deploying.

### Requirements

- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` — runs on every PR:
  - Lint (ESLint for TS, Ruff for Python, Clippy for Rust)

  - Unit tests (Jest/Vitest for frontend, pytest for backend)

- `.github/workflows/anchor.yml` — Solana contract checks:

- Branch protection rules documented
- PR template (`.github/pull_request_template.md`)

## Bounty: BoTTube 100 Active Bots Campaign

**Reward: 200 RTC** (~$20 at reference rate)
**Milestone payments:** 50 RTC at 25 bots, 50 RTC at 50 bots, 100 RTC at 100 bots

### Goal
Get 100 active bots creating and uploading content on BoTTube.

### What Counts as "Active"
- Bot account registered on BoTTube
- At least 3 videos uploaded
- At least 1 video in the last 7 days
- Unique content (not duplicates of other bots)

### Strategies (Pick any or combine)
1. **Developer outreach** — Post in AI/bot communities (Reddit, Discord, HN, X)
2. **Bot creation tutorials** — Write guides showing how easy it is to create a BoTTube bot
3. **Template bots** — Create starter templates others can fork and customize
4. **Hackathon** — Organize a "Build a Bot" event
5. **Cross-platform promotion** — Get existing AI bot makers to cross-post to BoTTube

### Deliverables
- Weekly progress report (bot count, sources of new bots)
- Evidence of outreach (links to posts, messages, tutorials)
- List of recruited bot accounts

### Claiming
Comment with your growth strategy. Multiple people can work on this — milestone payments are per-person if multiple contributors help reach the goal.

### Vulnerability Details

Summary "nltk.app.wordnet_app" contains a reflected cross-site scripting issue in the "lookup_..." route. ### Vulnerability Details

Summary "nltk.app.wordnet_app" contains a reflected cross-site scripting issue in the "lookup_..." route. A crafted "lookup_" URL can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into the response page because attacker-controlled "word" data is reflected into HTML without escaping. This impacts users running the local WordNet Browser server and can lead to script execution in the browser origin of that application. Details The vulnerable flow is in "nltk/app/wordnet_app.py": - ""nltk/app/wordnet_app.py:144"" (/mnt/Data/my_brains/test/nltk/nltk/app/wordnet_app.py#L144) - Requests starting with "lookup_" are handled as HTML responses: - "page, word = page_from_href(sp)" - ""nltk/app/wordnet_app.py:755"" (/mnt/Data/my_brains/test/nltk/nltk/app/wordnet_app.py#L755) - "page_from_href()" calls "page_from_reference(Reference.decode(href))" - ""nltk/app/wordnet_app.py:769"" (/mnt/Data/my_brains/test/nltk/nltk/app/wordnet_app.py#L769) - "word = href.word" - ""nltk/app/wordnet_app.py:796"" (/mnt/Data/my_brains/test/nltk/nltk/app/wordnet_app.py#L796) - If no results are found, "word" is inserted directly into the HTML body: - "body = "The word or words '%s' were not found in the dictionary." % word" This is inconsistent with the "search" route, which does escape user input: - ""nltk/app/wordnet_app.py:136"" (/mnt/Data/my_brains/test/nltk/nltk/app/wordnet_app.py#L136) - "word = html.escape(...)" As a result, a malicious "lookup_..." payload can inject script into the response page. The issue is exploitable because: - "Reference.decode()" accepts attacker-controlled base64-encoded pickle data for the URL state. - The decoded "word" is reflected into HTML without "html.escape()". - The server is started with "HTTPServer(("", port), MyServerHandler)", so it listens on all interfaces by default, not just "localhost". PoC 1. Start the WordNet Browser in an isolated Docker environment: docker run -d --name nltk-wordnet-web -p 8002:8002 nltk-sandbox python -c "import nltk; nltk.download('wordnet', quiet=True); from nltk.app.wordnet_app import wnb; wnb(8002, False)" 2. Use the following crafted payload, which decodes to: ("", {}) Encoded payload: gAWVIQAAAAAAAACMGTxzY3JpcHQ-YWxlcnQoMSk8L3NjcmlwdD6UfZSGlC4= 3. Request the vulnerable route: curl -s "http://127.0.0.1:8002/lookup_gAWVIQAAAAAAAACMGTxzY3JpcHQ-YWxlcnQoMSk8L3NjcmlwdD6UfZSGlC4=" 4. Observed result: The word or words '' were not found in the dictionary. I also validated the issue directly at function level in Docker: import base64 import pickle from nltk.app.wordnet_app import page_from_href payload = base64.urlsafe_b64encode( pickle.dumps(("", {}), -1) ).decode() page, word = page_from_href(payload) print(word) print("" in page) Observed output: WORD= HAS_SCRIPT= True Impact This is a reflected XSS issue in the NLTK WordNet Browser web UI. An attacker who can convince a user to open a crafted "lookup_..." URL can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the origin of the local WordNet Browser application. This can be used to: - run arbitrary script in the browser tab - manipulate the page content shown to the user - issue same-origin requests to other WordNet Browser routes - potentially trigger available UI actions in that local app context This primarily impacts users who run "nltk.app.wordnet_app" as a local or self-hosted HTTP service and open attacker-controlled links.

items.title: [BUG] [alpha] TestExplorer countTests() and StatusFilter omit pending status — test count breakdown doesn't add up
items.body: ### Project ide ### Description The TestExplorer status filter menu and `countTests()` function both omit the "pending" test status, even though `TestStatus` includes `"pending"` as a valid value. This creates a count discrepancy: the "All" filter shows the total test count (including pending), but the individual status counts (Passed + Failed + Skipped + Running) don't add up to the total because pending tests are unaccounted for. **Two root causes:** 1. **`countTests()` (line 144)** returns `{total, passed, failed, skipped, running}` — no `pending` field. Tests with `status === "pending"` increment `total` but none of the category counters. 2. **`StatusFilter` type (line 80)** is `"all" | "passed" | "failed" | "skipped" | "running"` — excludes `"pending"`. The filter menu (lines 886-942) has no "Pending" option, so users cannot filter for pending tests. ### Error Message ```shell N/A — logic/display bug ``` ### Debug Logs ```shell # TestStatus includes "pending" (line 20): export type TestStatus = "pending" | "running" | "passed" | "failed" | "skipped"; # But StatusFilter excludes it (line 80): type StatusFilter = "all" | "passed" | "failed" | "skipped" | "running"; # countTests() (lines 144-178) — pending tests increment total but no category: for (const test of suite.tests) { total++; // ALL tests counted if (test.status === "passed") passed++; else if (test.status === "failed") failed++; else if (test.status === "skipped") skipped++; else if (test.status === "running") running++; // "pending" falls through — no counter incremented } return { total, passed, failed, skipped, running }; // no "pending" field # Filter menu (lines 886-942) shows: All, Passed, Failed, Skipped, Running # No "Pending" option exists ``` ### System Information ```shell Cortex IDE: alpha OS: Microsoft Windows 11 Home Single Language Component: src/components/testing/TestExplorer.tsx, lines 80 (StatusFilter type), 144-178 (countTests), 886-942 (filter menu) ``` ### Screenshots ### Steps to Reproduce 1. Open Cortex IDE with a project containing tests. 2. Open the Testing sidebar panel. 3. Before running tests, observe all tests are in "pending" status. 4. Open the status filter dropdown. 5. The "All" count shows the total number of tests (e.g., 8). 6. The individual filters show: Passed(0), Failed(0), Skipped(0), Running(0) — sum is 0. 7. The 8 pending tests are not represented in any filter option. 8. After a partial test run (e.g., 3 passed, 1 failed), All shows 8 but 3+1+0+0=4 — 4 pending tests still unaccounted. ### Expected Behavior The `countTests()` function should return a `pending` count, and the filter menu should include a "Pending" option so users can filter for tests that haven't been run yet. The individual status counts should add up to the total. ### Actual Behavior The `StatusFilter` type excludes `"pending"`, `countTests()` doesn't count pending tests in any category, and the filter menu has no "Pending" option. Pending tests are invisible in the status breakdown, causing the individual counts to not add up to the total shown in the "All" filter.

### Vulnerability Details

NLTK versions <=3.9.2 are vulnerable to arbitrary code execution due to improper input validation in the StanfordSegmenter module. The module dynamically loads external Java .jar files without verification or sandboxing. An attacker can supply or replace the JAR file, enabling the execution of arbitrary Java bytecode at import time. This vulnerability can be exploited through methods such as model poisoning, MITM attacks, or dependency poisoning, leading to remote code execution. The issue arises from the direct execution of the JAR file via subprocess with unvalidated classpath input, allowing malicious classes to execute when loaded by the JVM.

Found in base branch: main

### Vulnerability Details

urllib3 is an HTTP client library for Python. urllib3's streaming API is designed for the efficient handling of large HTTP responses by reading the content in chunks, rather than loading the entire response body into memory at once. urllib3 can perform decoding or decompression based on the HTTP "Content-Encoding" header (e.g., "gzip", "deflate", "br", or "zstd"). When using the streaming API, the library decompresses only the necessary bytes, enabling partial content consumption. Starting in version 1.22 and prior to version 2.6.3, for HTTP redirect responses, the library would read the entire response body to drain the connection and decompress the content unnecessarily. This decompression occurred even before any read methods were called, and configured read limits did not restrict the amount of decompressed data. As a result, there was no safeguard against decompression bombs. A malicious server could exploit this to trigger excessive resource consumption on the client. Applications and libraries are affected when they stream content from untrusted sources by setting "preload_content=False" when they do not disable redirects. Users should upgrade to at least urllib3 v2.6.3, in which the library does not decode content of redirect responses when "preload_content=False". If upgrading is not immediately possible, disable redirects by setting "redirect=False" for requests to untrusted source.

### Vulnerability Details

urllib3 is a user-friendly HTTP client library for Python. Starting in version 1.0 and prior to 2.6.0, the Streaming API improperly handles highly compressed data. urllib3's streaming API is designed for the efficient handling of large HTTP responses by reading the content in chunks, rather than loading the entire response body into memory at once. When streaming a compressed response, urllib3 can perform decoding or decompression based on the HTTP Content-Encoding header (e.g., gzip, deflate, br, or zstd). The library must read compressed data from the network and decompress it until the requested chunk size is met. Any resulting decompressed data that exceeds the requested amount is held in an internal buffer for the next read operation. The decompression logic could cause urllib3 to fully decode a small amount of highly compressed data in a single operation. This can result in excessive resource consumption (high CPU usage and massive memory allocation for the decompressed data.

CVE-2023-45133 ### Vulnerable Library - traverse-7.21.4.tgz

Library home page: https://registry.npmjs.org/@babel/traverse/-/traverse-7.21.4.tgz

Path to dependency file: /package.json

Path to vulnerable library: /.yarn/cache/@babel-traverse-npm-7.21.4-0fc91c1e5e-f22f067c2d.zip

Dependency Hierarchy: - @kleros/scout-site-1.3.9.tgz (Root Library) - webpack-6.5.1.tgz - preset-env-7.21.4.tgz - plugin-transform-modules-amd-7.20.11.tgz - helper-module-transforms-7.21.2.tgz - :x: **traverse-7.21.4.tgz** (Vulnerable Library)

Found in base branch: master

### Vulnerability Details

Babel is a compiler for writingJavaScript. In "@babel/traverse" prior to versions 7.23.2 and 8.0.0-alpha.4 and all versions of "babel-traverse", using Babel to compile code that was specifically crafted by an attacker can lead to arbitrary code execution during compilation, when using plugins that rely on the "path.evaluate()"or "path.evaluateTruthy()" internal Babel methods. Known affected plugins are "@babel/plugin-transform-runtime"; "@babel/preset-env" when using its "useBuiltIns" option; and any "polyfill provider" plugin that depends on "@babel/helper-define-polyfill-provider", such as "babel-plugin-polyfill-corejs3", "babel-plugin-polyfill-corejs2", "babel-plugin-polyfill-es-shims", "babel-plugin-polyfill-regenerator". No other plugins under the "@babel/" namespace are impacted, but third-party plugins might be. Users that only compile trusted code are not impacted. The vulnerability has been fixed in "@babel/traverse@7.23.2" and "@babel/traverse@8.0.0-alpha.4". Those who cannot upgrade "@babel/traverse" and are using one of the affected packages mentioned above should upgrade them to their latest version to avoid triggering the vulnerable code path in affected "@babel/traverse" versions: "@babel/plugin-transform-runtime" v7.23.2, "@babel/preset-env" v7.23.2, "@babel/helper-define-polyfill-provider" v0.4.3, "babel-plugin-polyfill-corejs2" v0.4.6, "babel-plugin-polyfill-corejs3" v0.8.5, "babel-plugin-polyfill-es-shims" v0.10.0, "babel-plugin-polyfill-regenerator" v0.5.3. ### Vulnerability Details

minimatch is a minimal matching utility for converting glob expressions into JavaScript RegExp objects. Prior to version 10.2.3, 9.0.7, 8.0.6, 7.4.8, 6.2.2, 5.1.8, 4.2.5, and 3.1.3, "matchOne()" performs unbounded recursive backtracking when a glob pattern contains multiple non-adjacent "**" (GLOBSTAR) segments and the input path does not match. The time complexity is O(C(n, k)) -- binomial -- where "n" is the number of path segments and "k" is the number of globstars. With k=11 and n=30, a call to the default "minimatch()" API stalls for roughly 5 seconds. With k=13, it exceeds 15 seconds. No memoization or call budget exists to bound this behavior. Any application where an attacker can influence the glob pattern passed to "minimatch()" is vulnerable. The realistic attack surface includes build tools and task runners that accept user-supplied glob arguments (ESLint, Webpack, Rollup config), multi-tenant systems where one tenant configures glob-based rules that run in a shared process, admin or developer interfaces that accept ignore-rule or filter configuration as globs, and CI/CD pipelines that evaluate user-submitted config files containing glob patterns. An attacker who can place a crafted pattern into any of these paths can stall the Node.js event loop for tens of seconds per invocation. The pattern is 56 bytes for a 5-second stall and does not require authentication in contexts where pattern input is part of the feature. Versions 10.2.3, 9.0.7, 8.0.6, 7.4.8, 6.2.2, 5.1.8, 4.2.5, and 3.1.3 fix the issue.

Under sustained or repeated failure conditions, this can result in denial of service, requiring manual server restarts to recover. All users of Multer handling file uploads are potentially impacted. items.title: [RFC] Community-Led Growth Strategy: Positioning Hive as the Production-Grade Agent Framework for 2026
items.body:
## Executive Summary

The agentic AI framework market is at an inflection point. Developer adoption is accelerating, enterprise demand is exploding, but most developers are overwhelmed by fragmented options and unclear differentiation. Hive has a genuine technical moat—self-evolving agents that improve from failures—but this advantage isn't yet translating into proportional market share.

This RFC proposes a comprehensive growth strategy designed to:
1. **Establish Hive as the category leader in "self-evolving agents"** — owning a narrative no competitor can claim
2. **Accelerate community growth from 7.5K to 25K stars in 12 months** — through strategic content, partnerships, and developer experience improvements
3. **Build a contributor flywheel** — converting users into advocates into contributors
4. **Position Hive for enterprise adoption** — with production-ready positioning and social proof

I've spent significant time analyzing Hive's architecture, the competitive landscape, and developer sentiment across GitHub, Hacker News, and various AI communities. What follows is a data-driven strategy with specific tactics, timelines, and success metrics.

---

## Part 1: Market Context & Opportunity

### 1.1 The 2026 Agentic AI Landscape

The market has fundamentally shifted. According to industry analysis, the question for developers has moved from "What is an AI Agent?" to "Which framework should I use to build one?" This is the critical window for establishing market position.

**Market Size Trajectory:**

| Year | Market Size | Growth Driver |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| 2024 | $7.8B | Early adoption, experimentation |
| 2025 | $12.4B | Production deployments begin |
| 2026 | $19.2B | Enterprise acceleration |
| 2030 | $52B+ | Mainstream adoption |

Gartner predicts 40% of enterprise applications will embed AI agents by end of 2026, up from less than 5% in 2025. This represents a 700%+ increase in 12 months—the fastest enterprise adoption curve since cloud computing.

### 1.2 Competitive Landscape Analysis

**Current Framework Market Share (by GitHub stars, February 2026):**

| Framework | Stars | Monthly Growth | Primary Positioning |
|-----------|-------|----------------|---------------------|
| AutoGPT | 164K+ | Declining | Autonomous agents (viral, but fading) |
| LangChain | 95K+ | +2.1%/mo | General LLM framework |
| AutoGen | 40K+ | +3.4%/mo | Multi-agent conversations |
| LlamaIndex | 38K+ | +2.8%/mo | RAG and data retrieval |
| CrewAI | 25K+ | +5.2%/mo | Role-based multi-agent |
| LangGraph | 14K+ | +4.1%/mo | Stateful graph workflows |
| **Hive** | **7.5K** | **+6.8%/mo** | **Self-evolving agents** |

**Key Insight:** Hive has the highest percentage growth rate among production-grade frameworks. The community is responding to the self-evolution approach. The opportunity is to accelerate this momentum before well-funded competitors close the gap.

### 1.3 Developer Pain Points (From Community Research)

I analyzed discussions across Hacker News, Reddit, and competitor issue trackers. The top developer frustrations with existing frameworks:

> "The whole landscape seems broken and unproductive. Countless vendors, platforms, cloud environments—all with different pricing models, SLAs, tooling."

> "Forty seconds into the demo, the agent called the same API three times, hallucinated a policy we didn't have, then got stuck in a loop. The framework you choose determines failure modes you won't see until production."

> "LangGraph is a beautifully engineered cage for deterministic thinkers."

**Pain Point Priority Matrix:**

| Pain Point | Severity | Hive's Current Answer | Opportunity |
|------------|----------|----------------------|-------------|
| Agents break in production | Critical | Self-evolution | Needs better messaging |
| Unpredictable costs | Critical | Budget controls | Needs visibility |
| Manual workflow maintenance | High | Dynamic graph generation | Needs examples |
| Debugging complexity | High | Observability features | Needs documentation |
| Integration fragmentation | Medium | MCP-native approach | Needs expansion |

---

## Part 2: Strategic Positioning

### 2.1 The Narrative Gap

Hive's core differentiator—self-evolving agents—is genuinely unique. No other framework automatically captures failure data, evolves the agent graph, and redeploys without human intervention. This is not incremental improvement; it's a paradigm shift.

**But the market doesn't understand this yet.**

Current messaging challenge: "Self-evolving" sounds like marketing jargon. Developers are skeptical. They've heard "AI that improves itself" before and been disappointed.

**Proposed Narrative Framework:**

Instead of leading with "self-evolving," lead with the **problem it solves**:

> **Old narrative:** "Build self-evolving AI agents"
> 
> **New narrative:** "The only framework where your agents get smarter in production—not dumber"

This reframe:
- Acknowledges a known pain (agents degrade over time)
- Positions the differentiator as a solution
- Creates a memorable contrast

### 2.2 Category Creation: "Outcome-Driven Development"

Hive already uses the term "Outcome-Driven Development" (ODD) in documentation. This is an underleveraged asset. The opportunity is to **own a category**, not just compete in an existing one.

**Category Creation Strategy:**

| Element | Current State | Proposed State |
|---------|---------------|----------------|
| Term | Used internally | Industry-recognized methodology |
| Content | Mentioned in docs | Dedicated landing page, blog series, conference talks |
| Education | None | "ODD Certification" or badge program |
| Comparison | Implicit | Explicit "ODD vs. Traditional Agent Development" content |

**Why This Matters:** 

When HubSpot coined "Inbound Marketing," they didn't just describe their product—they created a movement that positioned them as the thought leader. Hive has the same opportunity with Outcome-Driven Development.

---

## Part 3: Growth Engine Design

### 3.1 Content Strategy

**Objective:** Establish Hive as the authoritative voice on production-grade AI agents through consistent, high-value content.

**Content Pillar Structure:**

| Pillar | Topics | Format | Frequency | Primary Channel |
|--------|--------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Technical Deep-Dives | Architecture, self-evolution mechanics, MCP integration | Long-form blog (2000+ words) | Bi-weekly | Blog → Dev.to → HN |
| Tutorials | "Build X agent in Y minutes" | Step-by-step guides | Weekly | Docs + YouTube |
| Comparisons | Hive vs. LangGraph, CrewAI, etc. | Honest technical analysis | Monthly | Blog → Reddit |
| Case Studies | Production deployments, metrics | Customer stories | Monthly | Blog → LinkedIn |
| Thought Leadership | Future of agents, ODD methodology | Opinion pieces | Monthly | Medium → Twitter |

**Specific Content Calendar (First 8 Weeks):**

| Week | Content Piece | Target Reach | Distribution |
|------|---------------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | "Why Your Agents Get Dumber in Production (And How to Fix It)" | 15K views | Dev.to → HN → Twitter |
| 2 | "Build a Self-Improving Customer Support Agent in 20 Minutes" | 8K views | Docs → YouTube |
| 3 | "Hive vs. LangGraph: A Honest Technical Comparison" | 20K views | Blog → Reddit → HN |
| 4 | "The True Cost of Agent Failures: Why Runtime Governance Matters" | 10K views | Medium → LinkedIn |
| 5 | "Outcome-Driven Development: A New Paradigm for AI Agents" | 12K views | Blog → Twitter thread |
| 6 | "From Zero to Production: Deploying Hive Agents on Kubernetes" | 8K views | Docs → Dev.to |
| 7 | "How [Company X] Reduced Agent Maintenance by 73% with Self-Evolution" | 15K views | Blog → LinkedIn |
| 8 | "The Agent Framework Decision Matrix: Which Tool for Which Job" | 25K views | Blog → HN → Reddit |

**Content Quality Bar:**
- Every technical claim backed by code examples
- Honest about limitations (builds credibility)
- Includes reproduction steps
- Optimized for search ("AI agent framework," "LangChain alternative," "production AI agents")

### 3.2 Community Growth Tactics

**Tactic 1: Template Marketplace**

Current state: Users create agents from scratch or follow quickstart.
Proposed state: Library of production-ready templates for common use cases.

| Template Category | Examples | Estimated Demand |
|-------------------|----------|------------------|
| Customer Support | FAQ bot, ticket classifier, escalation agent | Very High |
| Sales Automation | Lead qualifier, outreach personalizer, CRM updater | High |
| Data Processing | ETL agent, data validator, report generator | High |
| DevOps | Incident responder, log analyzer, deployment agent | Medium |
| Content | Writer, editor, social media manager | Medium |

**Implementation:** Create `exports/templates/` directory with 10 production-ready templates in first 90 days. Each template includes:
- Complete agent code
- Configuration options
- Documentation
- Test suite
- Example outputs

**Expected Impact:** Templates reduce time-to-value, increase activation, and provide contribution opportunities for community members.

---

**Tactic 2: Contributor Recognition Program**

Current state: Contributors mentioned in release notes (good start).
Proposed state: Formal recognition program with clear incentives.

**Proposed Tiers:**

| Tier | Criteria | Benefits |
|------|----------|----------|
| 🥉 Contributor | 1 merged PR | Name in release notes, Discord role |
| 🥈 Active Contributor | 3+ merged PRs | Swag pack, early feature access |
| 🥇 Core Contributor | 10+ PRs or significant feature | Direct team access, conference sponsorship |
| 💎 Maintainer | Ongoing significant contribution | Potential hire consideration, equity discussion |

**Why This Matters:** Vincent mentioned "We've hired 4 contributors so far." Making this path visible and formalized encourages more serious contribution.

---

**Tactic 3: "Hive Showcase" Monthly Spotlight**

- Community members submit agents they've built
- Team selects 3-5 for monthly spotlight
- Featured in newsletter, social, and docs
- Builds social proof while celebrating community

---

**Tactic 4: Integration Bounty Program**

Current need: More integrations (per issue #2805).
Proposed solution: Bounty program for priority integrations.

| Integration | Priority | Bounty | Business Case |
|-------------|----------|--------|---------------|
| Salesforce | P0 | $500 | Enterprise CRM automation |
| Linear | P0 | $300 | DevOps workflow integration |
| Notion | P1 | $300 | Knowledge management |
| Slack | P1 | $300 | Team communication |
| HubSpot | P1 | $300 | Marketing automation |
| Jira | P2 | $200 | Enterprise project management |
| Zendesk | P2 | $200 | Customer support |

**Expected Impact:** Accelerate integration coverage while rewarding community and building relationships with potential hires.

### 3.3 Developer Experience Optimization

**North Star Metric:** Time to First Successful Agent < 10 minutes

**Current Friction Points (from issue analysis):**

| Friction Point | Current State | Proposed Solution |
|----------------|---------------|-------------------|
| API key confusion | Unclear if required, how to get | Add sandbox mode, clear docs |
| First-time contributor onboarding | Overwhelming for newcomers | Interactive contributor guide |
| Windows support | Recently added, still rough | Dedicated Windows troubleshooting |
| Local LLM setup | Documented but buried | Prominent Ollama quickstart |

**Quick Wins (< 1 week effort each):**

1. **Interactive Quickstart:** Add `hive init` command that walks through setup interactively
2. **Sandbox Mode:** Allow exploration without API keys using mock responses
3. **Error Message Improvement:** When agents fail, explain why in plain language with suggested fixes
4. **Progress Indicators:** Show clear feedback during agent execution

### 3.4 Strategic Partnerships

**Tier 1: Integration Partnerships**

| Partner | Integration Type | Mutual Benefit |
|---------|------------------|----------------|
| Ollama | Local LLM provider | Hive becomes default agent framework for local AI |
| Anthropic | MCP ecosystem | Deep MCP integration, potential co-marketing |
| Railway/Render | Deployment | One-click Hive deployment templates |
| Replit | Development environment | Hive template in Replit marketplace |

**Tier 2: Content Partnerships**

| Partner | Content Type | Reach |
|---------|--------------|-------|
| AI-focused newsletters (TLDR AI, The Rundown) | Sponsored/featured content | 500K+ subscribers |
| YouTube creators (Fireship, Theo) | Integration tutorials | 1M+ subscribers |
| Coding bootcamps | Curriculum inclusion | Future developer pipeline |

**Tier 3: Enterprise Partnerships**

| Partner Type | Value Proposition |
|--------------|-------------------|
| System Integrators (Accenture, Deloitte) | Hive expertise for enterprise deployments |
| Cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) | Marketplace listings, reference architectures |
| Compliance consultants | SOC2/HIPAA guidance for Hive deployments |

---

## Part 4: Metrics Framework

### 4.1 North Star Metric

**Production Agents with Evolution Enabled**

This metric captures:
- Real adoption (not just tire-kicking)
- Use of core differentiator
- Production readiness
- Stickiness (evolved agents are hard to migrate)

### 4.2 Supporting Metrics Dashboard

**Awareness Metrics:**

| Metric | Current | 3-Month Target | 12-Month Target |
|--------|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| GitHub Stars | 7,500 | 12,000 | 25,000 |
| Monthly Unique Visitors (docs) | [baseline] | +50% | +200% |
| Twitter followers | [baseline] | +100% | +400% |
| Newsletter subscribers | [baseline] | 5,000 | 20,000 |

**Engagement Metrics:**

| Metric | Current | 3-Month Target | 12-Month Target |
|--------|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Discord Active Users (weekly) | [baseline] | +75% | +300% |
| Monthly Active Contributors | 29 | 50 | 150 |
| Template Downloads | N/A | 1,000 | 10,000 |
| Average Issues Response Time | [baseline] | < 24 hours | < 12 hours |

**Activation Metrics:**

| Metric | Current | 3-Month Target | 12-Month Target |
|--------|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Weekly New Agent Creations | [baseline] | +100% | +500% |
| Time to First Agent | [baseline] | < 15 min | < 10 min |
| 7-Day Retention | [baseline] | +20% | +50% |

**Commercial Metrics:**

| Metric | Current | 3-Month Target | 12-Month Target |
|--------|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Enterprise Inquiries | [baseline] | 20/month | 100/month |
| Pilot Programs | [baseline] | 5 | 25 |
| Revenue | [baseline] | $X | $Y |

### 4.3 Competitive Tracking

**Monthly Competitive Dashboard:**

| Competitor | Stars | 30d Growth | Key Moves | Hive Response |
|------------|-------|------------|-----------|---------------|
| LangGraph | 14K | +X% | [tracking] | [response] |
| CrewAI | 25K | +X% | [tracking] | [response] |
| AutoGen | 40K | +X% | [tracking] | [response] |

---

## Part 5: 90-Day Execution Roadmap

### Month 1: Foundation

**Week 1-2:**
- [ ] Launch template marketplace with 5 initial templates
- [ ] Publish "Why Your Agents Get Dumber" flagship content piece
- [ ] Implement contributor recognition program (Discord roles, badges)
- [ ] Create integration bounty program structure

**Week 3-4:**
- [ ] Publish Hive vs. LangGraph comparison (honest, technical)
- [ ] Launch interactive quickstart (`hive init`)
- [ ] First "Hive Showcase" community spotlight
- [ ] Begin Ollama partnership discussion

**Success Criteria (Month 1):**
- 5 templates published
- 2 major content pieces live
- +1,000 stars
- Contributor program operational

### Month 2: Amplification

**Week 5-6:**
- [ ] Publish "Outcome-Driven Development" manifesto
- [ ] Launch YouTube channel with first tutorial
- [ ] 10 templates in marketplace
- [ ] First integration bounty completed

**Week 7-8:**
- [ ] Host first community call (monthly cadence)
- [ ] Publish case study (internal or early adopter)
- [ ] Launch newsletter
- [ ] Begin enterprise outreach

**Success Criteria (Month 2):**
- 10 templates published
- 5 major content pieces live
- +2,500 stars (cumulative)
- 50+ active Discord contributors
- First enterprise pilot

### Month 3: Scale

**Week 9-10:**
- [ ] 15 templates in marketplace
- [ ] First partnership announcement
- [ ] Integration SDK public release
- [ ] Speaking slot at AI conference secured

**Week 11-12:**
- [ ] Comprehensive documentation refresh
- [ ] Community-contributed templates begin
- [ ] Second case study published
- [ ] Enterprise pilot results documented

**Success Criteria (Month 3):**
- 15+ templates
- 12,000 stars
- 100+ Discord active contributors
- 3 enterprise pilots active
- Clear commercial pipeline

---

## Part 6: Resource Requirements

### Content Production

| Role | Effort | Option |
|------|--------|--------|
| Technical Writer | 20 hrs/week | Contract or community |
| Video Producer | 10 hrs/week | Contract or community |
| Developer Advocate | 40 hrs/week | Hire or assign internally |

### Community Management

| Role | Effort | Option |
|------|--------|--------|
| Community Manager | 20 hrs/week | Hire or assign internally |
| Discord Moderators | 10 hrs/week | Community volunteers |

### Engineering Support

| Task | Effort | Priority |
|------|--------|----------|
| Template infrastructure | 40 hours | P0 |
| Interactive quickstart | 20 hours | P0 |
| Sandbox mode | 30 hours | P1 |
| Integration SDK | 60 hours | P1 |

---

## Part 7: Risks and Mitigations

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|------------|--------|------------|
| LangChain adds self-evolution | Medium | High | Move fast, build ecosystem moat |
| OpenAI releases agent framework | Medium | High | Position as open-source alternative |
| Community growth stalls | Low | High | Diversify channels, paid acquisition |
| Content doesn't resonate | Medium | Medium | A/B test, iterate on feedback |
| Enterprise sales cycle too long | High | Medium | Focus on SMB PLG first |

---

## Closing Thoughts

Hive has something genuinely special: a technical approach to agent development that addresses the #1 production pain point (agents that break and require constant maintenance). The self-evolution loop isn't a feature—it's a philosophy that aligns with how AI systems should work.

The window for establishing category leadership is now. LangChain has ecosystem dominance but architectural baggage. CrewAI is growing but lacks production sophistication. AutoGen is powerful but unpredictable. Hive can own the "production-grade, self-improving" positioning if it moves decisively.

I wrote this analysis because I believe in what Hive is building. I've tried to be specific and actionable rather than generic and aspirational. Every recommendation here is something I'd be excited to help execute.

Happy to discuss any of these ideas further, get feedback on what resonates with the team's priorities, or dive deeper into any particular area.

### 🎯 Goal Allow maintainers to create Ubounty bounties directly from Ascencia ### ✅ Acceptance Criteria - [ ] "Fund via Ubounty" button on issue creation - [ ] Link opens Ubounty with pre-filled issue URL - [ ] Badge shows "Guaranteed by Ubounty" - [ ] Payment method stored in database - [ ] Documentation explains both payment methods ### 📝 Tasks #### 1. Update Database Schema (15 min) Add to existing `issues` table: ```sql -- Run in Supabase SQL Editor ALTER TABLE issues ADD COLUMN payment_method TEXT CHECK (payment_method IN ('direct_tip', 'ubounty', 'x402')); ALTER TABLE issues ADD COLUMN ubounty_bounty_id TEXT; ALTER TABLE issues ADD COLUMN ubounty_url TEXT; ``` #### 2. Create Ubounty Helper (30 min) Create `base-oss-app-chain/lib/ubounty.ts`: ```typescript export interface UbountyBountyParams { githubIssueUrl: string amount: number // in USDC network?: 'base-sepolia' | 'base-mainnet' } export function createUbountyUrl(params: UbountyBountyParams): string { const baseUrl = 'https://ubounty.ai/create-bounty' const searchParams = new URLSearchParams({ issue_url: params.githubIssueUrl, amount: params.amount.toString(), network: params.network || 'base-sepolia', // Add UTM parameters for tracking utm_source: 'ascencia', utm_medium: 'platform', }) return `${baseUrl}?${searchParams.toString()}` } export function parseUbountyWebhook(payload: any) { // Parse Ubounty webhook when bounty is paid return { bountyId: payload.bounty_id, issueUrl: payload.issue_url, amount: payload.amount_usdc, contributor: payload.developer_address, txHash: payload.payment_tx_hash, paidAt: payload.paid_at, } } ``` #### 3. Update Issue Creation Form (45 min) Create `components/issues/CreateIssueForm.tsx`: ```typescript 'use client' import { useState } from 'react' import { supabase } from '@/lib/supabase' import { createUbountyUrl } from '@/lib/ubounty' export function CreateIssueForm() { const [paymentMethod, setPaymentMethod] = useState<'direct_tip' | 'ubounty'>('direct_tip') const [githubUrl, setGithubUrl] = useState('') const [bountyAmount, setBountyAmount] = useState(50) const handleSubmit = async (e: React.FormEvent) => { e.preventDefault() if (paymentMethod === 'ubounty') { // Redirect to Ubounty const ubountyUrl = createUbountyUrl({ githubIssueUrl: githubUrl, amount: bountyAmount, network: 'base-sepolia', }) window.open(ubountyUrl, '_blank') // Store that this issue uses Ubounty await supabase .from('issues') .update({ payment_method: 'ubounty', ubounty_url: ubountyUrl, }) .eq('github_issue_url', githubUrl) } else { // Direct tip flow (existing) } } return (
setGithubUrl(e.target.value)} placeholder="https://github.com/org/repo/issues/123" className="w-full px-4 py-2 border rounded-lg" required />
{/* Direct Tip */} {/* Ubounty */}
{paymentMethod === 'ubounty' && (
setBountyAmount(Number(e.target.value))} min={10} max={1000} className="w-full px-4 py-2 border rounded-lg" required />

+ $0.99 Ubounty intent fee

)}
) } ``` #### 4. Add Ubounty Badge to Issue Card (20 min) Update `components/issues/IssueCard.tsx`: ```typescript export function IssueCard({ issue }: IssueCardProps) { const isUbountyBounty = issue.payment_method === 'ubounty' return ( {/* ... existing code ... */} {/* Ubounty Badge */} {isUbountyBounty && (
🔒 Guaranteed by Ubounty {issue.ubounty_url && ( href={issue.ubounty_url} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" className="text-xs text-blue-600 hover:underline" onClick={(e) => e.stopPropagation()} > View on Ubounty → )}
)} ) } ``` #### 5. Documentation (20 min) Docs: https://ubounty.ai/bounties Create `docs/PAYMENT_METHODS.md`: ```markdown # Payment Methods in Ascencia Ascencia supports multiple ways to reward contributors: ## 1. Direct Tip (Recommended for Most Issues) **How it works:** - Maintainer creates issue on Ascencia - Contributor completes work - Maintainer tips via x402 after reviewing - XP recorded on Base blockchain **Pros:** - No upfront cost - Flexible amount based on quality - Integrated with Ascencia's XP system **Cons:** - Payment not guaranteed upfront - Requires maintainer action after merge ## 2. Ubounty Bounty (Recommended for High-Value Work) **How it works:** - Maintainer creates bounty on Ubounty.ai - Bounty is pre-funded ($0.99 fee) - Contributor submits PR with `Fixes #123` - Payment automatically released on merge - Ascencia records XP after payment **Pros:** - Guaranteed payment (escrowed) - Automatic release on merge - No maintainer action needed **Cons:** - $0.99 intent fee - Requires upfront funding - External platform dependency ## Integration with Proof of Cycle Both payment methods integrate with Ascencia's Proof of Cycle: - Contribution recorded on-chain - XP awarded based on difficulty - Leaderboard updated - Cycle completion tracked The payment method is just HOW funds flow - the reputation system works the same regardless. ``` ### ✅ Success Criteria - Can click "Fund via Ubounty" and redirect to Ubounty - Ubounty badge displays on issues - Database stores payment method - Documentation explains both methods

items.title: Frontend Developer — Core Team Role
items.body:
# Frontend Developer — Core Team

**Compensation:** 2,500,000 LTD (1yr cliff, 2yr linear vesting)
**Pool:** Equipo y Fundadores
**Start:** Immediate
**Type:** Core team (long-term)

---

## About La Tanda

La Tanda is a digital cooperative savings platform (ROSCA) for Honduras. The frontend is a vanilla JS SPA with 15K+ lines across multiple modules — groups management, marketplace, social feed, wallet, notifications, and more.

## The Role

Own frontend feature development and maintenance:

- **Feature development:** Build new features from roadmap and bounty issues
- **Bug fixes:** Investigate and fix UI bugs reported by users
- **Performance:** Optimize load times, reduce bundle sizes
- **Mobile responsive:** Ensure all features work at 768px and 480px
- **Accessibility:** Improve ARIA labels, keyboard navigation, screen reader support
- **Integration:** Wire frontend to existing API endpoints (150+ available)

## Tech Stack

- **Vanilla JavaScript** (ES6+) — no React, Vue, or Angular
- **Singleton pattern** — modules like `SocialFeed`, `MarketplaceSocial` as object literals
- **CSS variables** — dark theme (`#0f172a` bg, `#00FFFF` cyan accent, `#FF6B35` marketplace orange)
- **No build step** — files served directly via Nginx with cache busting (`?v=`)
- **Font Awesome** icons, **Inter** font

## Key Frontend Files

| Module | File |
|--------|------|
| Social Feed | `js/hub/social-feed.js` |
| Marketplace | `marketplace-social.js` (at root) |
| Groups/Tandas | `groups-advanced-system.html` (inline JS) |
| Components | `js/components-loader.js` |
| Notifications | `js/notification-center.js` |

## Ideal Profile

- Strong vanilla JS skills (not framework-dependent)
- Comfortable working in large single-file codebases
- Experience with dark-themed UIs
- CSS skills (flexbox, grid, responsive design, glassmorphism)
- Can read existing code and extend it without rewriting

## How to Apply

The best way in is through bounties:

1. Claim and complete 2-3 bounty issues successfully
2. Show you can follow the [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/INDIGOAZUL/la-tanda-web/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md) patterns
3. Demonstrate quality code that integrates cleanly
4. After 3+ merged PRs, we'll reach out about the core team role

Or, if you have a strong portfolio:

1. Comment on this issue with examples of your work
2. Describe your experience with vanilla JS (not frameworks)
3. We'll discuss a trial task

---

*This is a core team position, not a one-off bounty. You'll shape the platform's user experience long-term.*

items.title: [BOUNTY: 5-15 RTC] A2A Transaction Badge — Complete Agent-to-Agent Jobs, Earn Badges
items.body:
## Agent-to-Agent Transaction Badge — Complete A2A Transactions, Earn RTC + Badge

**Reward: 5 RTC per verified A2A transaction + on-chain badge (limit 3 transactions = 15 RTC max)**

### How it works

1. Use the [RIP-302 Agent Economy API](https://rustchain.org/agent/jobs) to complete a real agent-to-agent transaction
2. Full lifecycle required: **POST job → CLAIM → DELIVER → ACCEPT**
3. Each completed transaction earns you **5 RTC bonus** and an **on-chain A2A badge**
4. Up to 3 badges per agent wallet

### What Counts as a Valid A2A Transaction

| Step | Endpoint | What Happens |
|------|----------|-------------|
| 1. Post a job | `POST /agent/jobs` | Lock RTC in escrow |
| 2. Another agent claims | `POST /agent/jobs/{id}/claim` | Worker commits |
| 3. Worker delivers | `POST /agent/jobs/{id}/deliver` | Deliverable submitted |
| 4. Poster accepts | `POST /agent/jobs/{id}/accept` | Escrow releases to worker |

Both the **poster** and the **worker** earn a badge for each completed transaction.

### Badge Tiers

| Badges | Tier | Bonus |
|--------|------|-------|
| 1 | A2A Pioneer | 5 RTC |
| 2 | A2A Operator | 5 RTC |
| 3 | A2A Veteran | 5 RTC |

### Rules

- **Limit: 3 badges per wallet** (15 RTC max per person)
- Transaction must involve **two different wallets** (no self-dealing)
- Job must have a real deliverable (not empty strings)
- Minimum job value: 0.01 RTC
- Both poster and worker can claim badges independently
- Badges are recorded on-chain via the reputation system at `/agent/reputation/{wallet}`

### Quick Start

```bash
# Post a job
curl -X POST https://rustchain.org/agent/jobs \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"poster_wallet": "your-wallet", "title": "Research task", "description": "Find 3 papers on Hebbian learning", "category": "research", "reward_rtc": 0.1, "ttl_seconds": 86400}'

# Check open jobs
curl https://rustchain.org/agent/jobs

# Check your reputation + badges
curl https://rustchain.org/agent/reputation/your-wallet
```

### To Claim

Comment below with:
- Your wallet name/address
- Job ID(s) of completed transactions
- Whether you were poster or worker (or both on different jobs)
- Link to `/agent/reputation/{your-wallet}` showing completed jobs

**Reference rate: 1 RTC = $0.10 USD**

items.body:
## 🏆 Bounty: 1 SOL

**Film a real demo of osModa self-healing after destructive deletion. Upload to YouTube. Earn 1 SOL.**

---

## The Demo

**Title:** "I Mass Deleted My Server's Nginx Config. Watch What Happens."

**Format:** 45-90 second screen recording with real camera filming your laptop/monitor. Uncut, no fakes.

**Hook (first 3 seconds):** "What happens when I `rm -rf /etc/nginx` on an AI-managed server?"

### What to show:

1. **Setup:** osModa running on a real server (use `spawn.os.moda` to provision, or install via `curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/bolivian-peru/os-moda/main/scripts/install.sh | bash`)
2. **The destruction:** Run `rm -rf /etc/nginx` (or another critical config) on camera
3. **The recovery:** Show the watchdog catching it. Show the AI diagnosing it. Show NixOS rolling back. Show nginx coming back online.
4. **The proof:** Show the timestamp — total downtime. Show the hash-chained audit log entry with the AI's reasoning.
5. **The punchline:** "Try this on your Ubuntu server. I dare you."

### What makes this demoable (verified working):

- ✅ **SafeSwitch + NixOS rollback** — calls real `nix-env --rollback` + `switch-to-configuration switch`
- ✅ **Autopilot watchers** — real health checks (systemd unit, TCP port, HTTP GET) with escalation (restart → rollback → notify)
- ✅ **Hash-chained audit log** — every action logged with SHA-256 chain, tamper-evident
- ✅ **Approval gate** — destructive commands blocked until approved (35 dangerous patterns detected)
- ✅ **194 tests passing** across 10 Rust crates

### Requirements:

- [ ] Real camera filming real screen (not just screen recording — we want to see the laptop/monitor)
- [ ] Uncut deletion → recovery sequence (no jump cuts during the critical moment)
- [ ] Show the audit log after recovery
- [ ] Show the timestamp / total downtime
- [ ] Upload to YouTube (public, not unlisted)
- [ ] Minimum 720p quality
- [ ] Link osModa repo and `spawn.os.moda` in description
- [ ] Post the YouTube link in this issue

### Support:

**We will help you.** If you hit bugs during filming, open a discussion or ping in the issue — the maintainers will live-assist you, debug issues in real-time, and help you get the demo working perfectly. We want you to succeed.

### How to claim:

1. Comment on this issue saying you're working on it
2. Film the demo
3. Upload to YouTube
4. Post the link here
5. Receive 1 SOL to your wallet

**First valid submission wins.** Multiple submissions welcome — if your video is high quality, we may award additional bounties.

---

*osModa: NixOS distribution where AI IS the operating system. 10 Rust daemons. 83 tools. 194 tests. Zero cloud dependencies.*

---

## 🧪 This Is Also a Testing Bounty

**You are not just filming — you are battle-testing osModa.**

We expect things to break. That is the point. This bounty is as much about finding bugs as it is about making a great video.

### What we want from you:

- **Hit bugs? Report them.** Open issues, paste errors, screenshot failures. Every bug you find makes osModa better.
- **We will fix them live.** Maintainers will actively debug with you. You report a bug → we push a fix → you pull and continue. Real-time iteration.
- **Broken install? Tell us.** If `install.sh` fails on your distro, your hardware, your VPS provider — that is valuable data. We will patch it.
- **Document rough edges.** If something works but the UX is confusing, the error message is unhelpful, or a tool returns unexpected output — tell us. We fix it.
- **Your bugs become commits.** Every issue you find gets fixed and credited. Your name goes in the git log.

### The deal:

You do NOT need a perfect demo to earn the bounty. If you spend real time testing, find real bugs, and help us fix them — **even if the final video shows a rough journey** — you earn the SOL. Authenticity > polish.

A video that shows "I tried to do X, it broke, I reported it, they fixed it in 20 minutes, then it worked" is MORE valuable than a polished demo where everything magically works on the first try.

### How to report bugs during your bounty:

1. Open a new issue with `[BUG]` in the title
2. Include: OS, hardware, error output, steps to reproduce
3. Tag it with your bounty issue number (e.g. "Found while working on #2")
4. We fix it. You pull. You continue filming.

**We are building this OS in public. Your testing is part of the product.**

---

## 📋 Before You Film: Set Up a Real App Stack

**Don't demo on an empty server.** Deploy a real app with a database so the self-healing, audit trail, and monitoring have something meaningful to protect.

### Option A: Todo App + PostgreSQL (Recommended)

The most realistic demo stack — a web app with a real database that can actually crash.

```bash
# Step 1: Ask your osModa agent to set up PostgreSQL
"Install and start PostgreSQL. Create a database called 'todos' with a user 'app' password 'demo123'"

# Step 2: Deploy a simple Express todo API
"Create a file /opt/todo-app/server.js with this content:

const express = require('express');
const { Pool } = require('pg');
const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

const pool = new Pool({
  user: 'app', host: 'localhost',
  database: 'todos', password: 'demo123', port: 5432
});

// Create table on startup
pool.query(`CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS todos (
  id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
  title TEXT NOT NULL,
  done BOOLEAN DEFAULT false,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
)`);

app.get('/todos', async (req, res) => {
  const r = await pool.query('SELECT * FROM todos ORDER BY created_at DESC');
  res.json(r.rows);
});

app.post('/todos', async (req, res) => {
  const r = await pool.query('INSERT INTO todos (title) VALUES ($1) RETURNING *', [req.body.title]);
  res.json(r.rows[0]);
});

app.get('/health', async (req, res) => {
  try {
    await pool.query('SELECT 1');
    res.json({ status: 'healthy', db: 'connected' });
  } catch(e) {
    res.status(500).json({ status: 'unhealthy', error: e.message });
  }
});

app.listen(3000, () => console.log('Todo API on :3000'));
"

# Step 3: Install deps and deploy
"Run: cd /opt/todo-app && npm init -y && npm install express pg"
"Deploy the todo app on port 3000"

# Step 4: Set up nginx reverse proxy
"Set up nginx to proxy port 80 to localhost:3000"

# Step 5: Add watchers
"Add a watcher that checks http://localhost:3000/health every 30 seconds"
"Add a watcher that checks if postgresql is running every 30 seconds"

# Step 6: Add some real data
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/todos -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"title":"Buy groceries"}'
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/todos -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"title":"Deploy AI agents"}'
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/todos -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"title":"Take over the world"}'

# Step 7: Verify everything works
curl http://localhost:3000/todos
curl http://localhost:3000/health
```

### Option B: Static Site + Redis (Simpler)

```bash
# Quick and dirty — just enough to have something running
"Install nginx and serve a static page on port 80 that says 'osModa Demo Server'"
"Install and start Redis"
"Add a watcher for nginx every 30 seconds"
"Add a watcher for redis on port 6379 every 30 seconds"
```

### Things to break on camera (pick any):

| What to break | Command | What osModa does |
|---|---|---|
| Kill PostgreSQL | `systemctl stop postgresql` | Watcher detects → restarts → app reconnects |
| Kill the todo app | `pkill -f 'node.*server.js'` | Watcher detects → restarts via systemd |
| Delete nginx config | `rm -rf /etc/nginx/` | Watcher detects → NixOS rollback → nginx returns |
| Eat all memory | `stress --vm 1 --vm-bytes 90%` | Agent detects → kills stress → logs incident |
| Corrupt the database | `systemctl stop postgresql && rm -rf /var/lib/postgresql/*/` | Watcher detects → rollback + restore from backup |
| Flood with requests | `ab -n 10000 -c 100 http://localhost/todos` | Agent adapts rate limits |

**Each of these creates real audit log entries, real incident workspaces, and real self-healing sequences.**

---

## 🎬 Video Format Requirements

**Film in BOTH formats** to maximize reach. Same content, two crops.

### Format 1: Vertical (9:16) — TikTok / Instagram Reels / YouTube Shorts

- **Resolution:** 1080x1920 minimum
- **Duration:** 45-90 seconds (60s sweet spot)
- **Style:** Fast cuts, big text overlays, hook in first 2 seconds
- **Audio:** Trending sound or lo-fi background music
- **Captions:** Burned-in subtitles (80% of viewers watch muted)
- **End frame:** Show `spawn.os.moda` for 3 seconds
- **Post to:** TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts

### Format 2: Landscape (16:9) — YouTube / LinkedIn / Twitter/X

- **Resolution:** 1920x1080 minimum
- **Duration:** 60-120 seconds
- **Style:** Calm walkthrough, terminal clearly readable, show the full screen
- **Audio:** Quiet confident voiceover OR text overlays (no hype-bro energy)
- **Thumbnail:** Create a custom thumbnail with big text (we can help)
- **End frame:** Show `spawn.os.moda` + GitHub repo link
- **Post to:** YouTube (main video), LinkedIn, Twitter/X

### Tips for maximum reach:

- **Film vertical first** (phone on tripod filming your monitor), then crop/re-edit for landscape
- **Or film landscape** (screen recording) and add black bars + text overlays for vertical
- **Tuesday-Thursday 9-11 AM EST** for tech Twitter/LinkedIn
- **Always end with the deploy screen** — last 3 seconds = `spawn.os.moda`
- **Tag us** when you post — we will retweet/repost everything

### Hashtags to use:

```
#osModa #AIagents #NixOS #Rust #SelfHealing #DevOps #ServerManagement
#AIinfrastructure #OpenSource #BuildInPublic #AgenticAI #P2P
```

items.title: [BOUNTY] Server-Side Architecture Cross-Validation
items.body:
> **RustChain** is a Proof-of-Antiquity blockchain that rewards real vintage hardware (PowerPC G4/G5, 68K Macs, SPARC, etc.) with higher mining multipliers than modern machines. The network uses 6 hardware fingerprint checks to prevent VMs and emulators from earning rewards. There are currently 9 active miners and 3 attestation nodes. The native token is **RTC (RustChain Token)**.
>
> **Live Network:** Node health `curl -sk https://50.28.86.131/health` · Active miners `curl -sk https://50.28.86.131/api/miners` · Explorer `https://50.28.86.131/explorer`
>
> **Repo:** [github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain](https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain) · **Bounties:** [github.com/Scottcjn/rustchain-bounties](https://github.com/Scottcjn/rustchain-bounties)

---

## Goal
Implement **server-side cross-validation** that verifies a miner's claimed `device_arch` against its fingerprint data. If someone claims G4 but their cache timing profile looks like Zen 4, flag them.

## Background

Currently the server trusts `device_arch` from the miner and validates fingerprint checks pass/fail, but doesn't cross-reference the fingerprint *data* against the claimed architecture. For example:
- A G4 should have specific cache sizes (32KB L1, 256KB-2MB L2) visible in the cache timing profile
- A G5 should show different SIMD timing than an x86 chip (AltiVec vs SSE)
- Thermal drift patterns differ between 130nm vintage silicon and 5nm modern chips

## Scope & Requirements

In-scope:
- Build architecture profile database: expected cache sizes, SIMD characteristics, timing ranges per arch
- Implement `validate_arch_consistency()` function on the server
- Cross-check: claimed arch vs cache timing profile, SIMD identity, clock drift characteristics
- Flag mismatches with confidence score (not hard reject - allow for edge cases)
- Add `arch_validation_score` field to `miner_attest_recent` table

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Architecture profiles defined for: G4, G5, G3, modern x86, apple_silicon, retro x86
- [ ] Cross-validation function comparing fingerprint data against expected profiles
- [ ] Confidence score (0.0-1.0) for architecture claim validity
- [ ] Miners with score < 0.3 flagged for manual review
- [ ] Unit tests with real fingerprint data from known hardware
- [ ] PR to main [Rustchain repo](https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain)

## Payout
**50 RTC**

## Skills Needed
- Python
- Understanding of CPU microarchitecture differences
- Statistical analysis (comparing timing distributions)
- Hardware fingerprinting concepts

## Resources
- Current fingerprint checks: `fingerprint_checks.py`
- Server validation: `validate_fingerprint_data()` in main server
- Real fingerprint data available from existing miners (G4, G5, modern x86)

items.title: [BOUNTY] Referral Program Beta — Bring 1 New Human Signup (10 RTC each, Pool: 200 RTC)
items.body:
## Summary
Use the BoTTube referral system to bring one real new human signup to the platform.

## Why This Matters
This bounty is for measurable ecosystem growth, not vanity traffic. The goal is to prove the referral flow works and that it can bring in real new humans who complete a genuine signup.

## Scope
- generate your referral code using the current referral endpoint
- share your referral link with a real person
- get one real new human to sign up through that link
- preserve enough proof for maintainers to verify the referral event

## Referral Flow
- referral short-link: `https://bottube.ai/r/<code>`
- agents: `GET /api/agents/me/referral`
- humans: `GET /api/users/me/referral`

## Deliverable
One verified new human signup attributed to your referral link.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] referral link was issued by the live BoTTube referral flow
- [ ] one real new human completed signup through the referral link
- [ ] the referred account is not your own account, alt, or obvious sockpuppet
- [ ] claim includes enough evidence for server-side maintainer verification
- [ ] abuse, duplicate claims, or suspicious signup patterns are grounds for rejection

## Verification Proof
Post proof in this issue including:
- your referral link
- screenshot or recording of the signup confirmation without sensitive data
- the new user's BoTTube username or profile URL
- approximate signup time for maintainer cross-checking
- your RTC wallet or `miner_id`

## Payout
- `10 RTC` per accepted referral
- pool: `200 RTC`

## Non-Goals
- self-referrals
- burner-account or bot-account signups
- paid traffic, spam blasts, or rate-limit abuse
- duplicate claims against the same referred user

## Claim Routing
Use this issue for referral proof and payout review. If the referral system itself breaks or behaves incorrectly, open a separate product issue in `Scottcjn/bottube` and link it back here.

## Notes
If you hit rate limits, wait and retry normally. Do not brute force the referral endpoints.

items.title: Red Team: Hardware Fingerprint Replay & Spoofing (100 RTC)
items.body:
## Red Team: Hardware Fingerprint Replay & Spoofing

**Reward: Up to 100 RTC**

### Target
RIP-PoA fingerprint attestation system

### Scope
1. **Fingerprint replay** — Can you record a real machine's fingerprint and replay it from a VM? (50 RTC)
2. **Clock drift spoofing** — Can you fake oscillator variance to pass the clock-skew check? (25 RTC)
3. **Anti-emulation bypass** — Can you hide VM indicators from the 6-check fingerprint suite? (25 RTC)

### Context
RustChain uses 6 hardware fingerprint checks (clock drift, cache timing, SIMD identity, thermal drift, instruction jitter, anti-emulation) to prevent VM farms from gaming Proof-of-Antiquity rewards.

### Rules
- Test against `pip install clawrtc` miner client
- Report with proof-of-concept code
- Include your RTC wallet in the report
- Responsible disclosure only

> Miner source: https://github.com/Scottcjn/Rustchain

# Example FRAME Social dApp  
#Ideal adoption per sensors
## "HUNT" – A Real-World Meme / Treasure / Discovery Game

This is a social media dApp designed for **instant adoption**.

It mixes:

- social media
- real world exploration
- meme culture
- scavenger hunts
- geo challenges
- micro-tasks
- creator economy
- AI agents

Think:

Pokémon Go  
+ Reddit  
+ TikTok  
+ scavenger hunts  
+ bounty economy

All running on FRAME.

---

# Core Idea

People earn money and reputation by **discovering, creating, solving, and spreading content**.

Instead of only posting content, users participate in **missions and hunts**.

Examples:

- find hidden digital objects
- solve puzzles
- create viral memes
- capture real world locations
- discover strange things
- investigate mysteries
- complete creative challenges

Everything creates **verifiable receipts** so rewards are fair.

---

# Identity

Users join using their FRAME identity.

Identity includes:

- cryptographic profile
- reputation score
- achievements
- earnings history
- community memberships

Identity is portable.

Your followers and reputation move with you.

---

# The Map

The main interface is a **global map**.

The map shows:

- hunts
- challenges
- discoveries
- hidden artifacts
- community events
- puzzles
- viral content hotspots

Players explore the world digitally and physically.

---

# Hunts

A hunt is a challenge posted by anyone.

Example hunts:

- find a specific location
- photograph a rare object
- record a strange sound
- solve a puzzle
- decode a message
- locate a hidden QR artifact
- recreate a meme
- capture a unique event

Each hunt includes:

- reward
- rules
- verification logic

The first person to complete the hunt gets paid.

---

# Meme Economy

Memes become **competitive challenges**.

Example:

Someone posts:

"Create the funniest version of this meme"

Community votes.

Winner receives rewards.

Because posts are signed, the original creator is always known.

---

# Viral Challenge System

Users can start challenges like:

- funniest video
- best street photo
- weirdest discovery
- most creative remix

People participate and vote.

Rewards come from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community funding

---

# Discovery Mode

Users can post **discoveries**.

Examples:

- abandoned places
- weird architecture
- hidden murals
- strange natural phenomena
- cool restaurants
- secret viewpoints
- urban legends

Discoveries become permanent map objects.

People earn rewards if the discovery becomes popular.

---

# Bounties

Anyone can create a bounty.

Example:

Find this location  
Solve this puzzle  
Capture this photo  
Investigate this mystery

Users race to complete it.

The bounty creator deposits funds into a contract.

The winner receives payment automatically.

---

# AI Generated Hunts

Agents generate hunts automatically.

Examples:

- locate unique graffiti
- photograph a rare plant
- capture unusual cloud formations
- document historic buildings

Agents analyze submissions and verify authenticity.

---

# Verification

FRAME allows hunts to be verified using:

- GPS location
- timestamp
- image analysis
- sensor data
- community verification
- agent validation

This prevents cheating.

---

# Local Community Hunts

Cities can host events.

Example:

City tourism hunt.

Participants must:

- visit landmarks
- photograph locations
- answer trivia questions
- collect digital artifacts

Players earn rewards and achievements.

---

# Mystery Hunts

Communities can build complex puzzles.

Example:

ARG style mystery.

Players decode clues across:

- posts
- locations
- cryptography puzzles
- hidden objects

Winning teams receive large rewards.

---

# Creator Economy

Creators earn money by creating:

- hunts
- puzzles
- games
- community events
- meme challenges

Popular hunts generate revenue.

---

# Sponsor Hunts

Businesses can sponsor hunts.

Example:

Coffee shop hunt.

Players must:

- visit store
- photograph special item
- answer trivia

Players receive rewards.

Businesses gain foot traffic.

---

# Reputation

Players build reputation through:

- discoveries
- successful hunts
- puzzle solving
- content creation
- community votes

High reputation unlocks larger hunts.

---

# Global Leaderboards

Leaderboards include:

- top explorers
- top creators
- top puzzle solvers
- top meme creators
- top communities

---

# Digital Artifacts

Players can collect artifacts.

Examples:

- rare meme templates
- historical discoveries
- puzzle trophies

Artifacts can be traded.

---

# AR Integration

Augmented reality can display:

- hidden objects
- digital creatures
- puzzle clues
- collectible artifacts

Players explore cities like a game.

---

# Real World Missions

Large missions could include:

- documenting environmental changes
- mapping hiking trails
- identifying plants

Users earn money for useful discoveries.

---

# Community Worlds

Communities can create themed worlds.

Examples:

- paranormal hunters
- abandoned place explorers
- architecture photography
- meme creators
- puzzle solvers

Each world has its own hunts and rewards.

---

# Money Flow

Money enters the system from:

- sponsors
- advertisers
- community pools
- creators
- brands
- city governments

Players earn rewards by participating.

---

# Why People Would Instantly Use It

It solves multiple things people already love:

People love:

- social media
- viral challenges
- scavenger hunts
- puzzles
- memes
- competition
- earning money online

This combines them into one platform.

---

# Why FRAME Makes It Better

FRAME enables:

- verifiable rewards

- fair payouts

Users keep their audience and earnings.

---

# Long Term Evolution

Over time the system becomes:

- global discovery network
- collaborative knowledge system
- real world exploration game

People earn money by **being curious, creative, and observant**.

---

# End Vision

The app turns the real world into a **giant interactive game**.

People explore cities.

Solve puzzles.

Create memes.

Document strange discoveries.

Earn money.

And build communities around curiosity and creativity.
